coredata.dc:title: Selective and sensitive detection of hypochlorite in living cells using reduced iodine-doped carbon dots
coredata.prism:copyright: © 2024 The Author(s). Published by Elsevier Ltd.

coredata.dc:creator: Zhou, Xinyue
Zhang, Shiyi
Lv, Jian
Wang, Xiaoyuan
Gong, Lijuan
Gao, Yating
Chang, Shuai
Chen, Bin Bin
Li, Dawei
Qian, Ruocan
coredata.dc:description: Hypochlorite (ClO−) is an important metabolite that participates a series of intracellular signal communications, which is closely related with the generation and development of various diseases. Herein, we design and prepare reduced iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) that are highly sensitive to ClO−. The proposed I-CDs show bright green fluorescence (FL), which can be efficiently quenched upon treatment with ClO− because of the destroy of I-CDs structure caused by oxidation of iodine by ClO−. This sensing platform displays a good linear response range of 1 − 60 μM with a low detection limit of 95.1 nM. Importantly, monitoring ClO− level in living cells is further realized, demonstrating the capability of this strategy to determine the content of intracellular ClO−. This work provides a promising tool for visual sensing ClO−, which is of great significance for early diagnosis of ClO− related diseases.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Carbon dots
Iodine doping
Hypochlorite
Turn-off detection
Living cells

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974046, 21977031, 22176058), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M701195), Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (19520744000, 22ZR1416800, 23ZR1416100), the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B16017), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201717003). The authors thank Research Center of Analysis and Test of East China University of Science and Technology for the help on the characterization.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of I-CDs. (a) TEM image and (b) Raman spectrum of I-CDs. (c) Emission spectra of I-CDs at different excitations. (d) The absorption, maximum excitation, and maximum emission spectra of I-CDs (inset: the photograph of the I-CDs solution under a 365 nm UV lamp). (e) XPS spectrum of I-CDs. (f) FT-IR spectra of I-CDs, APBA, iohexol, and EDA.

Stability and detection of I-CDs. (a) Viability of HeLa cells treated with the different concentrations of I-CDs (5 − 200 μg/mL) after 8 h incubation. (b) Stability of I-CDs in a salty medium. (c) Stability of I-CDs in different pH solution. (d) Sensitivity of ClO− detection. F0: Initial FL intensity of I-CDs. F: FL intensity of I-CDs after the addition of ClO− with different concentrations. (e) FL spectra of I-CDs in the presence of different interfering substances (100 μM). From a to m: H2O2; HCO3 −; K+; Mg2+; Na+; SO4 2−; mannose; urea; threonine; Cl−; ClO−; glucose; Ca2+. (f) F0-F value of the I-CDs corresponding to (e). F0: Initial FL intensity of I-CDs. F: FL intensity of I-CDs after the addition of interfering substances. Data are expressed as mean ± standard error of three experiments.
Quenching mechanism of I-CDs. (a) Absorption spectra of I-CDs before and after the addition of ClO−. (b) High-resolution I3d XPS spectra of I-CDs before and after the addition of ClO−.

Incubation condition optimization of I-CDs. (a) Confocal microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with I-CDs (30 μg/mL) for different times (0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 h). Color bar: 0–180 from bottom to top. (b) Confocal microscopic images of HeLa cells incubated with different concentrations of I-CDs (5, 10, 20, 30, and 50 μg/mL) for 2 h. Color bar: 0–180 from bottom to top. (c) FL intensity histogram of I-CDs in (a). (d) FL intensity histogram of I-CDs in (b). Data are expressed as mean ± standard error of three experiments. The intensity values represent the average FL intensity of all cells in the image.

Confocal microscopic images of HeLa cells when adding different concentrations of ClO− ((a) 10 μM, (b) 30 μM, and (c) 50 μM) at different times. Color bar: 0–180 from bottom to top. Statistical histogram of FL intensity when adding different concentrations of ClO− ((d) 10 μM, (e) 30 μM, and (f) 50 μM) at different times. F0: Initial FL intensity of I-CDs. F: FL intensity of I-CDs after the addition of ClO− at different times.
Schematic diagram showing the synthesis process of I-CDs and the detection of ClO− by I-CDs in living cells.

head.ce:title: Selective and sensitive detection of hypochlorite in living cells using reduced iodine-doped carbon dots

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory for Advanced Materials, Shanghai Key Laboratory of Functional Materials Chemistry, Feringa Nobel Prize Scientist Joint Research Center, Frontiers Science Center for Materiobiology & Dynamic Chemistry, School of Chemistry & Molecular Engineering, East China University of Science and Technology

ce:note-para.ce:italic: These authors contributed equally to this work.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Hypochlorite (ClO−) is an important metabolite that participates a series of intracellular signal communications, which is closely related with the generation and development of various diseases. Herein, we design and prepare reduced iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) that are highly sensitive to ClO−. The proposed I-CDs show bright green fluorescence (FL), which can be efficiently quenched upon treatment with ClO− because of the destroy of I-CDs structure caused by oxidation of iodine by ClO−. This sensing platform displays a good linear response range of 1 − 60 μM with a low detection limit of 95.1 nM. Importantly, monitoring ClO− level in living cells is further realized, demonstrating the capability of this strategy to determine the content of intracellular ClO−. This work provides a promising tool for visual sensing ClO−, which is of great significance for early diagnosis of ClO− related diseases.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: This work developed the reduced iodine-doped carbon dots (I-CDs) for in situ measurement of ClO− level in living cells.

ce:section.ce:para: Reactive oxygen species (ROS) are a group of highly reactive chemicals containing oxygen produced either exogenously or endogenously, which widely exist in nature and organisms [1–3]. The common ROS include superoxide and hydroxyl radicals, nitric oxide, singlet oxygen, nitrogen dioxide, hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), and hypochlorite (ClO−). As the by-product of cell metabolism, ROS play essential roles in cell physiology and participate in a variety of pathological processes [4]. Elevated ROS level may lead to molecular damage, known as “oxidative stress”, and will also affect gene expression, inducing cell apoptosis and the subsequent generation of inflammation or even tumor [5,6]. As an important member of ROS family, ClO− has attracted increased attention in recent years due to its strong pathogenicity, which is a key factor causing lung injury, neurasthenia and cancer [7–9]. Therefore, developing a powerful probe for sensitive and specific detection of ClO− in living cells is of great importance for early diagnosis of ClO− related diseases.

Owing to the significance of ClO− in biological research, a variety of strategies have been reported for detection of ClO−, including iodine titration, electrochemical analysis, colorimetry, and high-performance liquid chromatography [10–13]. However, these traditional methods are complex in operation and time-consuming, which limits their applications in biological detection and living cell imaging. Because of its advantages in visualization analysis and rapid response, fluorescence (FL) detection has been emerged as an advanced sensing technique [14–18]. The design concept of fluorescent probes responding to ClO− mainly focuses on integrating reducing atoms or groups into the probe structure, such as S/Se/Te atoms [19–21], hydroquinone [7], and N–OH structural units [22]. These reducing atoms or groups can be oxidized by the ClO−, causing the changes in probe structure and leading to a transition of FL signals. While these fluorescent molecule probes face many challenges posed by poor water solubility, complex synthesis, poor photostability, and high toxicity.

Different from traditional organic dyes and semiconductor quantum dots, carbon dots (CDs) as new type of luminescent carbon nanomaterials has been extensively studied in the field of sensing because of their various unique advantages [23–25]. Firstly, CDs are mainly composed of carbon and oxygen elements, thus they display lower toxicity compared to toxic heavy-metal-based quantum dots, which allow cells or organisms to live sustainably even at high concentrations. Meanwhile, CDs have good water solubility and dispersibility in aqueous solution due to the abundant hydrophilic functional groups on their surface. Importantly, CDs show more superior photostability without the concerns of photobleaching compared to easily photo-quenched dyes, which are beneficial for long-term imaging. In addition, the characteristics of easy preparation and low cost have also promoted the application of CDs in a wider range of fields. Considering the reducibility of iodine, iodine-related probes can theoretically interact with ClO− with oxidizing properties to achieve the detection of ClO−. While the currently reported iodine-doped CDs (I-CDs) are mainly used for photocatalysis [26], nanozyme [27], and CT imaging [28], and the research on ROS detection by I-CDs is still in a vague stage.

APBA, iohexol, EDA, sodium hypochlorite (NaClO), sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3), sodium chloride (NaCl), potassium chloride (KCl), magnesium chloride (MgCl2), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4), calcium chloride (CaCl2), D-glucose, D-mannose, urea, and threonine are obtained from Aladdin (Shanghai, China). Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2, 30 wt%) is bought from Shanghai Titan Scientific Co., Ltd. Commercial filters (0.22 μm) are from Lab Instrument Co., Ltd (Tianjin, China). The dialysis membrane (100–500 Da) for the purification of I-CDs is commercially available from Solarbio Co., Ltd. (Beijing, China). Cell proliferation and cytotoxicity assay kit are purchased from Sangon Biotech CO., Ltd (Shanghai, China). The confocal dishes (diameter = 20 mm) are purchased from Biosharp. The cell culture bottles (T25) and cell culture dishes are purchased from NEST Biotechnology, China. Phosphate buffered saline (PBS) buffer solution, Dulbecco's Modified Eagle Medium (DMEM) medium, fetal bovine serum, penicillin, streptomycin, and pancreatin used for the cells culture are obtained from Sangon Biotech, Shanghai. HeLa cervical cancer cell is purchased from FuHeng Cell Center (Shanghai, China). All aqueous solutions are prepared by 18.2 mΩ cm ultrapure water (EMD Millipore, TONDINO, Shanghai).

All reagents are weighed by an analytical balance (ME 104, METTLER TOLEDO). The morphology characterization of the as-synthesized I-CDs is obtained using a high-resolution transmission electron microscope (TEM). Fourier transform infrared (FT-IR) spectrum of I-CDs is recorded on a Hitachi FTIR-8400S spectrometer. A 759 S UV–vis spectrophotometer is used for UV–vis absorption characterization of I-CDs. The FL spectra can be measured through a FL spectrophotometer. The Raman spectrum of I-CDs is determined using a Thermo Scientific DXR2xi Raman imaging microscope. The element composition of I-CDs is measured with a Thermo Scientific Escalab 250Xi X-ray photoelectron spectrometer (XPS). An inverted microscope (Eclipse TiU, Nikon, Japan) equipped with a 40× objective lens (NA = 0.8) and a white light source (100 W halogen lamp) were used to obtain the images of cells before cell experiments. The cell images were obtained on a TCS SP5 laser scanning confocal microscope (Leica, Germany).

I-CDs are prepared by a facile hydrothermal synthesis strategy. In detail, 0.1 g of iohexol, 0.1 g of APBA, and 200 μL of EDA are mixed and dissolved into 5 mL ultrapure water. The above mixture solution is then transferred to a 25 mL Teflon-lined stainless-steel autoclave and reacted at 200 °C for 12 h. The final appearance of reddish-brown solution in the autoclave indicates the successful preparation of I-CDs.

I-CDs obtained by hydrothermal reaction should be purified before the subsequent application. First, I-CDs solution is filtered by the commercial filter (0.22 μm). After that, the obtained solution is injected into the cellulose ester dialysis membrane and placed in the ultrapure water for 2 days. Finally, I-CDs powder can be obtained by freeze-drying the solution and dissolved again at a concentration of 100 mg/mL as a mother liquor. The I-CDs solution is stored in a 4 °C refrigerator for further characterization and application.

First, we dilute the I-CDs solution (mother liquor) 50 times with ultrapure water to obtain 2 mg/mL of I-CDs solution. Then, 200 μL diluted I-CDs solution is mixed with 1800 μL NaClO solution with different concentrations. The mixed solution is transferred to the quartz cell for the FL detection. The selectivity of the ClO− detection is further investigated by detecting the FL intensity of I-CDs in the presence of 100 μM different interfering substances (H2O2, HCO3 −, K+, Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2-, mannose, urea, threonine, Cl−, glutamate, Ca2+).

The HeLa cells are cultured in DMEM medium supplemented with 10% fetal bovine serum, penicillin (100 units/mL), and streptomycin (100 units/mL). The culture environment is in a humid atmosphere at 37 °C with 5% CO2. I-CDs in living HeLa cells are imaged using a laser confocal microscope (λex = 488 nm and λem = 500 − 560 nm for the green channel).

Specifically, after overnight culture, HeLa cells are seeded at a density of 5 × 103 in confocal dishes. In the investigation of the effects of incubation time of I-CDs on cell imaging, HeLa cells are separately incubated with 30 μg/mL of I-CDs for different time (0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 h). Subsequently, the culture medium is removed, and the cells are washed three times with PBS buffer (pH = 7.4) before adding fresh DMEM medium for laser confocal imaging. Additionally, another group of HeLa cells are incubated with different concentrations of I-CDs (5, 10, 20, 30, and 50 μg/mL) for 2 h, following the same procedure as described above.

Briefly, HeLa cells are seeded in 96-well plates at a density of 5 × 103 cells per well and incubated overnight. Then, the culture medium is discarded and replaced with the fresh culture medium containing I-CDs solution of different concentrations (5, 10, 20, 30, 50, 80, 100, and 200 μg/mL). After incubation for 6 h, 100 μL fresh medium and 10 μL Cell Counting Kit-8 (CCK-8 solution) are added to each well, and then incubated for another 1 h. The 96-well plates are shaken for 2 min at a low speed. The zero adjustment well (DMEM medium, CCK-8 solution) and control well (HeLa cells, DMEM medium, CCK-8 solution) are set for the following absorbance test (three wells for each group).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization and optical properties of I-CDs
ce:section.ce:para:
TEM images clearly show the size of I-CDs ranging from 7 − 9 nm by the particle size statistic (
Fig. 1 a and S2). As shown in Fig. 1 b, Raman spectrum is determined to further confirm the structure of I-CDs. The D band is corresponding to defects and disordered sp3-hybridized carbon, while the G band represents ordered sp2-hybridized carbon [29]. The intensity ratio of these two bands (I
D /I
G ) is calculated to 1.23, which indicates that the I-CDs have a highly disorder structure. The proposed I-CDs show an excitation-independent green FL emission under the excitation of 390 − 460 nm ( Fig. 1 c and S3), and the maximum emission is about at 512 nm when excited by 410 nm, and I-CDs have a strong UV absorption at 229 nm because of the π–π* transition ( Fig. 1 d). Meanwhile, the relative quantum yield of the I-CDs is about 2.7% ( Fig. S4 ), demonstrating good potential for optical imaging.

Subsequently, the cytotoxicity of I-CDs is evaluated by the CCK-8 assay. As shown in Fig. 2 a, the average cell viability using different concentrations of I-CDs is higher than 80%, indicating the good biocompatibility of I-CDs. Stability is also an important parameter of the optical nanoprobe. Our results show that FL intensities of I-CDs are almost unchanged, whether in NaCl medium ( Fig. 2 b) or different pH solution ( Fig. 2 c). Besides, the FL emission of I-CDs keeps stable under UV light treatment for 60 min ( Fig. S6 ), revealing the excellent resistance to photobleaching. These results confirm that I-CDs have a great stability and have the potential as the nanoprobe for living cells imaging.

Furthermore, the selectivity of the ClO− detection is also investigated. As shown in Fig. 2 e, a series of biologically relevant species including H2O2, HCO3 −, K+, Mg2+, Na+, SO4 2−, mannose, urea, threonine, Cl−, glucose, Ca2+ are added into the I-CDs solution, and none of them can influence the FL emission, indicating the good selectivity. Subsequently, other methods are used to compare with our proposed I-CDs probes ( Table S2 ). Results show that the proposed I-CDs exhibit superior sensitivity, and the LOD is one order of magnitude lower than most reported probes. Importantly, I-CDs have the fastest response speed to ClO− compared to other probes, and their FL emission can be immediately quenched once ClO− is added. These results reveal that the destruction of I-CDs structure by the oxidation of iodine sites is a very powerful strategy for achieving rapid and sensitive detection of ClO−. Moreover, compared to fluorescent molecular probes, the prepared I-CDs have the characteristics of low toxicity, good water solubility, and superior photostability, making them suitable for long-term imaging of ClO− in living cells.

Considering the good stability and biocompatibility, I-CDs are suitable for the detection of intracellular ClO−. HeLa cells are selected as the model cell, and incubated with I-CDs. Meanwhile, HeLa cells only have a weak auto-fluorescence ( Fig. S13 ), indicating that the detection of ClO− by I-CDs is almost unaffected by auto-fluorescence. To eliminate the effect of incubation conditions on intracellular ClO− sensing, the incubation time (0.5, 1, 2, 3, and 4 h) is firstly explored. As shown in Fig. 4 a and c, HeLa cells show a green FL emission after incubation with I-CDs (30 μg/mL) for 0.5 h, and the FL intensity increases with the increasing incubation time and then reaches a platform after 2 h. Thus, 2 h is selected as the most appropriate time, indicating the cellular uptake process of I-CDs is comparably rapid and efficient. Subsequently, the concentration of I-CDs is optimized. As shown in Fig. 4 b and d, the FL intensity of HeLa cells increases as the concentration of I-CDs increases from 5 to 50 μg/mL. The FL intensity remains almost unchanged when I-CDs concentration exceeds 30 μg/mL, indicating that the 30 μg/mL of I-CDs is the optimal concentration.

To prove the feasibility of ClO− detection by the I-CDs in vivo, the dynamic progress of ClO− entering into HeLa cells in real time is monitored. As shown in Fig. 5, I-CDs nanoprobes (30 μg/mL) can enter cells and produce green FL in the absence of ClO−. After the addition of 10, 30, and 50 μM ClO− in situ, the FL intensity decreases over time and the higher the concentration of ClO−, the lower the FL intensity of cells ( Fig. 5 a-c). The corresponding pseudo color images clearly show concentration- and time-dependent FL quenching. After incubation for 1 h, different concentrations of ClO− can reduce the FL intensity by 23% (10 μM ClO−), 55% (30 μM ClO−), and 85% (50 μM ClO−), respectively ( Fig. 5 d-f). In view of this, the proposed I-CDs can be well used for in situ measurement of ClO− level in living HeLa cells.

In summary, we synthesize I-CDs with bright green FL by one-pot hydrothermal method using three precursors (APBA, iohexol, and EDA). The proposed I-CDs exhibit numerous advantages, including facile preparation, excellent stability, and low toxicity. Importantly, I-CDs have a fast response to ClO− with good sensitivity and selectivity. Meanwhile, the I-CDs can be used to detect the intracellular ClO− by dynamically monitoring the FL change, which provides a great potential on the detection of ClO− in vivo. This work not only elucidates the mechanism of I-CDs probes in ClO− detection, but also provides a powerful tool for early diagnosis of ClO− related diseases.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This research was supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (21974046, 21977031, 22176058), the China Postdoctoral Science Foundation (2021M701195), Shanghai Science and Technology Committee (19520744000, 22ZR1416800, 23ZR1416100), the Program of Introducing Talents of Discipline to Universities (B16017), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities (222201717003). The authors thank Research Center of Analysis and Test of East China University of Science and Technology for the help on the characterization.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.nxmate.2024.100220.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Fluorescent copper nanoclusters for the iodide-enhanced detection of hypochlorous acid

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Investigation of sp2-carbon pattern geometry in boron-doped diamond electrodes for the electrochemical quantification of hypochlorite at high concentrations

Macpherson, Investigation of sp2-carbon pattern geometry in boron-doped diamond electrodes for the electrochemical quantification of hypochlorite at high concentrations, ACS Sens. 5 (2020) 789-797. https://doi.org/10.1021/acssensors.9b02444.

sb:title.sb:maintitle: Green-emitting carbon quantum dots as a dual-mode fluorescent and colorimetric sensor for hypochlorite
